walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Firework Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Hanabi. First, find all fifty firework cards. There are five colors: white, red, blue, yellow, and green. Each color has three ones, two twos, two threes, two fours, and one five. Give the deck a good shuffle and place it face down in the center of the table.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Cards
walkthrough.text: Now here is the twist that makes Hanabi unique. Deal cards to each player, but everyone must hold their cards facing outward, away from themselves. You can see everyone else's cards but you cannot see your own. For two or three players, deal five cards each. For four or five players, deal four cards each.

walkthrough.title: Place the Clock Tokens
walkthrough.text: Find the eight blue clock tokens. Place them in the center of the table with the clock side facing up. These tokens are spent when giving hints to other players. When all eight are used, no more hints can be given until someone discards to recover one.

walkthrough.title: Place the Fuse Tokens
walkthrough.text: Now find the three red fuse tokens and place them in the center. Each time someone plays a card that does not fit on a firework pile, one fuse is removed. If all three fuses are gone, the fireworks explode and everyone loses immediately. Think of these as your three strikes.

walkthrough.title: Leave Space for Firework Piles
walkthrough.text: Clear a visible area in the center of the table for five firework piles, one per color. These piles will be built up from one to five during the game. Right now they are empty. The goal is to complete all five piles for a perfect score of twenty-five.

walkthrough.title: Choose First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to go first. Hanabi recommends the most colorfully dressed player, but any method works. Play will go clockwise from the first player. Remember, do not look at your own cards! You are ready to start the fireworks show.

summary.title: Shuffle the Firework Cards
bullets: 50 cards in 5 colors (white, red, blue, yellow, green)
Per color: three 1s, two 2s, two 3s, two 4s, one 5
Shuffle and place face down centrally

summary.title: Deal the Cards
bullets: Hold cards OUTWARD — you cannot see your own hand
2-3 players: 5 cards each; 4-5 players: 4 cards each

summary.title: Place the Clock Tokens
bullets: 8 blue tokens in center, clock side up
Spent to give hints; recovered by discarding

summary.title: Place the Fuse Tokens
bullets: 3 red tokens in center
Lose 1 per failed play; all 3 gone = game over

summary.title: Leave Space for Firework Piles
bullets: 5 piles (1 per color), built from 1→5
Start empty; goal = complete all 5 for max 25 points

summary.title: Choose First Player
bullets: Any selection method; play clockwise
Do NOT look at your own cards

walkthrough.title: The Goal
walkthrough.text: Hanabi is a cooperative game where everyone works together to build five firework displays, one per color, from one to five. The catch is that you cannot see your own cards. Your teammates give you hints about what you are holding. Together you try to score as close to twenty-five points as possible.

walkthrough.title: Your Three Action Choices
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you must do exactly one of three things. You can give a hint to another player, which costs one blue clock token. You can play a card from your hand onto a firework pile. Or you can discard a card to recover one blue clock token. Let's go through each one.

walkthrough.title: Giving a Hint
walkthrough.text: To give a hint, flip one blue clock token to its used side. Then point to one other player and tell them about their cards. You must choose either a color or a number. For example, you might say 'these two cards are blue' or 'this card is a three.' You must point to every card in their hand that matches. You cannot give a hint that matches zero cards.

walkthrough.title: Playing a Card
walkthrough.text: To play a card, choose one from your hand and place it face up. Remember, you cannot see your own cards, so you are relying on hints and memory. If the card is the next number needed for its color pile, it stays. For example, a blue three goes on a blue pile showing one and two. If the card does not fit, it goes to the discard pile and one red fuse token is removed.

walkthrough.title: Completing a Firework
walkthrough.text: When you successfully play the five for any color, that firework is complete. As a bonus, one used blue clock token is flipped back to available. This reward encourages you to finish fireworks because it gives your team more hints to work with.

walkthrough.title: Discarding a Card
walkthrough.text: To discard, remove a card from your hand without looking at it first and place it face up in the discard pile. Then flip one used blue clock token back to available, recovering a hint for the team. Finally, draw a new card from the deck. Discarding is how you manage your hint supply, but be careful because discarded cards might have been needed.

walkthrough.title: Drawing Cards
walkthrough.text: After playing or discarding a card, draw one new card from the deck. Hold it facing outward so you cannot see it but everyone else can. If the deck is empty, you do not draw. Once the deck runs out, each player gets exactly one more turn before the game ends.

walkthrough.title: The Three Fuses
walkthrough.text: Every time a played card does not fit on its firework pile, one red fuse token is removed. If the third fuse is removed, the fireworks explode and the game ends immediately with a score of zero. Three strikes and you are out. Be careful about playing cards you are not sure about.

walkthrough.title: Card Uniqueness Warning
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to the card distribution. Each five exists only once per color. If you discard a five, that color can never be completed. The ones are safest to discard because there are three copies of each. Twos, threes, and fours have two copies each. The discard pile is public information, so always check it before discarding.

walkthrough.title: End of Game and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends in one of three ways. If all three fuses are lost, you score zero. If all five fireworks are completed, you score a perfect twenty-five. If the deck runs out, each player takes one final turn, then tally the highest card played in each of the five piles. Your total is your score. The rating scale goes from horrible at zero to five, up to legendary at twenty-five.

summary.title: The Goal
bullets: Cooperative: build 5 firework piles (1→5), one per color
Cannot see own cards; rely on teammate hints
Max score: 25 (all 5 fireworks complete)

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Fireworks Show
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Hanabi! We are going to walk through a practice round with three players. Remember, everyone holds their cards facing outward. You can see your teammates' cards but not your own. This feels strange at first, but you will get used to it quickly.

walkthrough.title: Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Each player has five cards held outward. Take a moment to look at your teammates' hands. You can see what everyone else has. Notice any ones? Those need to be played first since every firework starts at one. Mentally note where the important cards are.

walkthrough.title: Player One Gives a Hint
walkthrough.text: Player one sees that player two has a red one and a blue one. Ones are the most important cards early in the game because nothing can be played without them. Player one flips a blue clock token and says, 'these two cards are ones,' pointing to both cards. Now player two knows the positions of their ones.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Plays a Card
walkthrough.text: Player two was just told about two ones. They pick the leftmost one and place it face up. It is the red one! It goes on the empty red pile as the first card. Success! Player two draws a new card from the deck and adds it to their hand, facing outward.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Gives a Color Hint
walkthrough.text: Player three sees that player one has a green one. They flip a blue token and say, 'this card is green,' pointing to the single green card. Player one now knows they have a green card. Combined with the game state showing no green firework started, player one can deduce it is probably the green one.

walkthrough.title: Player One Plays Green
walkthrough.text: Player one plays the green card. It is the green one! Another firework pile started. Two out of five colors now have their foundation. Player one draws a replacement card. The team is off to a great start.

walkthrough.title: Tracking Blue Tokens
walkthrough.text: Let's check the hint supply. Two blue tokens have been used for hints. Six remain. The team needs to be mindful of this. If hints run out, no one can give information and players are forced to discard or play blind. Managing the hint economy is crucial.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Playing Without Enough Info
walkthrough.text: Player two remembers they were told about a second one in their hand. They play it confidently. But wait. It is the blue one, and the blue pile already had a one played earlier in this example. Oh no, that card does not fit because the blue one was already played! A fuse is lost. Player two assumed the card was needed without checking the board state.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check the Board
walkthrough.text: Before playing any card, always check what has already been played on the firework piles. Knowing you have a 'one' is not enough. You need to know which color one, and whether that color's pile already has a one. Combine hint information with the current board state before playing.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Discarding a Five
walkthrough.text: Player three has no useful hints to give and the team is low on blue tokens. They decide to discard a card to recover a token. They pick a card at random and it turns out to be the yellow five. That card only existed once. The yellow firework can now never be completed. Maximum possible score just dropped from twenty-five to twenty.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Protect the Fives
walkthrough.text: Fives are irreplaceable. Each color has only one five. If a five is discarded, that firework can never reach completion. The whole team should prioritize hinting about fives as soon as they spot one. If someone hints your card is a five, never discard it. Treat fives like precious gems.

walkthrough.title: Smart Discarding
walkthrough.text: When you must discard, choose wisely. If you have no information about a card, discard from the oldest position, typically the rightmost card in your hand. Cards that have received no hints are less likely to be critical. Always check the discard pile first to see what has already been lost.

walkthrough.title: Recovering Momentum
walkthrough.text: The team has used several hints and lost a fuse, but they are not out yet. Player one gives a carefully chosen hint about player three's hand, identifying a white two. Player three knows the white one has already been played, so they can safely play the white two next turn. The team is back on track.

walkthrough.title: Building the Fireworks
walkthrough.text: Over the next several turns, the team continues giving hints, playing cards, and occasionally discarding to recover tokens. The key rhythm of Hanabi emerges. Hint, play, hint, play. Occasionally discard when tokens run low. The firework piles grow as the team coordinates.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Wasting Hints
walkthrough.text: Player one uses a hint to tell player two about a card that cannot be played for several turns. That hint spent a precious blue token without enabling an immediate play. Meanwhile, player three had a card that could have been played right now with a single hint. Timing matters.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Hint for Immediate Plays
walkthrough.text: Prioritize hints that enable immediate plays. A hint that lets someone play a card this turn is far more valuable than a hint about a card that cannot be played yet. Save your tokens for high-impact moments. Every blue token is a team resource, not just yours.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have walked through a practice game of Hanabi. You have seen how to give effective hints, play cards safely, and manage the token economy. Remember the three lessons. Always check the board before playing. Protect the fives at all costs. And prioritize hints that enable immediate plays.

walkthrough.title: Before You Light the Fuse
walkthrough.text:
Before you play a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for hint conventions and token management strategies.

Second, reference the Appendix for card distribution and scoring details.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Let's start with the traps new players fall into. First, playing cards with incomplete information. Just knowing a card is a 'one' does not mean it is needed right now. Second, discarding fives or last copies of critical cards. Third, spending hints on information that does not lead to immediate plays. Fourth, forgetting to check the discard pile before deciding what to keep. And fifth, running out of blue tokens by hinting too aggressively without discarding.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Getting Ones Out
walkthrough.text: Every firework starts at one, so getting all five ones played is the most important early goal. Focus your first hints on identifying ones in other players' hands. Once the ones are down, the team has a foundation to build on. If ones are stuck in players' hands unknowingly, the entire game stalls.

walkthrough.title: Hint About Playable Cards
walkthrough.text: The most valuable hints are the ones that tell a player exactly what to play this turn. If the red pile shows one and two, and you can see a red three in someone's hand, hint about it. A hint that leads to an immediate play is worth far more than general information about cards that cannot be used yet.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Discard Pile
walkthrough.text: The discard pile is public and contains vital information. Before discarding, check if the card you are about to lose is the last copy of its type. If both copies of a blue three have been discarded, the blue firework can never get past two. Use the discard pile to inform your plays, discards, and hint priorities.

walkthrough.title: Token Management
walkthrough.text: Eight blue tokens sounds like a lot, but they disappear fast. A good rule of thumb is to keep at least two tokens in reserve at all times. When tokens run low, someone needs to discard to recover one. Plan your discards proactively rather than being forced into them at a bad time. Completing a five also returns one token, which is a valuable bonus.

walkthrough.title: Develop Group Conventions
walkthrough.text: Experienced Hanabi teams develop unspoken conventions to make hints more efficient. For example, many groups agree that hinting a number means 'play this card,' while hinting a color means 'do not discard this.' These conventions are not in the rules, but they dramatically improve communication. Discuss with your group what conventions to use before starting.

walkthrough.title: Discard Order Strategy
walkthrough.text: When you have no useful information about your cards and need to discard, use a consistent order. Many groups agree to discard from the oldest position, which is typically the rightmost card. Newest cards go on the left. This way, if someone gives you a hint about a specific card, the team knows you will not accidentally discard it before acting on the hint.

walkthrough.title: Protect Critical Cards
walkthrough.text: Certain cards are more critical than others. Fives are irreplaceable. The last copy of any card is dangerous to lose. When you see a critical card in someone's hand and it is in a discard-vulnerable position, hint about it immediately, even if it cannot be played yet. Preventing a critical loss is sometimes more important than enabling a play.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't play with incomplete info — check board state first
Don't discard 5s or last copies of critical cards
Don't spend hints on non-actionable information
Don't ignore the discard pile or let blue tokens run dry

Use discard info for play, discard, and hint decisions

Plan discards proactively to recover tokens
Completing a 5 returns 1 token as bonus

summary.title: Protect Critical Cards

Preventing a loss can be more important than enabling a play

walkthrough.title: Finesse Plays
walkthrough.text: The finesse is an advanced technique where you give a hint to one player that indirectly communicates information to a third player. For example, if player A has a red two and player B has a red three, you hint the red three to player B. Player A, seeing no red two on the board, deduces that they must have the red two and plays it first. This saves a hint and creates a chain of plays.

walkthrough.title: Reverse Hints
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the most valuable hint is about what a card is not. If you hint 'these cards are blue' while pointing to three of five cards, the player knows the other two cards are not blue. This negative information can be just as useful as positive information. Experienced teams track both what they know and what they can rule out about each card.

walkthrough.title: The Discard Signal
walkthrough.text: In many experienced groups, discarding a specific card position carries meaning. Discarding your newest card can signal that the team should not worry about your hand. Discarding from an unusual position can indicate urgency about other cards. These signals emerge naturally as groups play together and develop shared understanding.

walkthrough.title: Multi-Turn Planning
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think multiple turns ahead. If you need to play a blue two, a blue three, and a blue four in sequence, plan the hints to set up this chain. Give the blue two hint first so that card can be played, which unlocks the three, which unlocks the four. Timing your hints to create cascading plays is how you achieve high scores.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Token Counting
walkthrough.text: When the deck is nearly empty, count remaining cards and hints carefully. Each player gets exactly one final turn after the last card is drawn. Calculate how many plays the team needs to finish as many fireworks as possible. Sometimes it is better to discard for tokens early so you can give critical hints in the final turns.

walkthrough.title: Sacrifice Plays
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the mathematically correct play is to intentionally burn a fuse. If you are nearly certain a card is playable but cannot verify, playing it and potentially losing a fuse might be worth it if the team has fuses to spare and the alternative is wasting two hints. This is a calculated risk, not recklessness.

walkthrough.title: Card Counting
walkthrough.text: Advanced Hanabi players mentally track which cards remain in the deck. With fifty cards distributed across known hands, the discard pile, played piles, and the unseen deck, you can calculate probabilities. If two of three white ones are visible and one must be in the remaining deck or someone's hand, that narrows possibilities significantly.

walkthrough.title: Pursuing the Perfect Score
walkthrough.text: A perfect twenty-five requires completing all five fireworks. This means zero wasted fuses, zero critical card losses, and maximum hint efficiency. It requires the team to work in perfect coordination from start to finish. Most groups aim for a consistent twenty or higher rather than gambling everything on perfection. Know when good enough is the right target.

summary.title: Finesse Plays
bullets: Hint Player B's card to indirectly inform Player A
Player A deduces they must have the connecting card
Saves a hint and creates a chain of plays

summary.title: Reverse Hints
bullets: Pointing to 3 of 5 cards tells player the other 2 are NOT that type
Negative information is as useful as positive
Track both known and ruled-out information per card

summary.title: The Discard Signal
bullets: Discard position can carry implicit meaning
Newest card discard = hand is safe
Signals develop naturally as groups play together

summary.title: Multi-Turn Planning

Time hints to create sequential play chains
Requires thinking 2-3 turns ahead

1 final turn per player after last draw
Sometimes discard early to bank tokens for critical final hints

summary.title: Sacrifice Plays
bullets: Intentionally burn a fuse when probability favors it
Worth it if fuses to spare and alternative wastes 2+ hints
Calculated risk, not recklessness

summary.title: Card Counting

Remaining cards narrow probabilities

summary.title: Pursuing the Perfect Score
bullets: Perfect 25 requires zero wasted fuses and zero critical losses
Demands perfect coordination from start to finish
Consistent 20+ is often a better target than gambling on perfection

entries.definition: One of eight tokens spent to give hints. Recovered by discarding a card or completing a firework (playing a 5). Public resource shared by the entire team.
One of three tokens lost when a played card does not fit its firework pile. If all three are lost, the game ends immediately with a score of zero.
One of five color-specific card piles built from 1 to 5. Each pile starts empty and grows as correct cards are played. Completed pile = 5 points.
Action that costs one blue token. Tell one player about all cards of a specific color OR number in their hand. Must indicate every matching card. Cannot hint zero cards.
Action where you place a card from your hand onto a firework pile. If the card is the next number needed for its color, it stays. Otherwise, it is discarded and a fuse is lost.
Action where you remove a card from your hand to the discard pile, recovering one blue token. Then draw a replacement card. Cannot look at the card before discarding.
Per color: three 1s, two 2s, two 3s, two 4s, one 5. Fifty cards total across five colors. The 5 is unique and irreplaceable per color.
Two to three players hold five cards each. Four to five players hold four cards each. Cards are always held facing outward.
The signature Hanabi rule: hold your cards facing away from you so you cannot see them. Other players can see your hand. You rely on hints and deduction.
When giving a hint, you must point to every card that matches the stated color or number. Partial hints are not allowed. This ensures hints always convey full information.
Playing the 5 card of any color. Bonus: recovers one blue clock token. All five completed = perfect score of 25.
When the draw deck runs out, each player gets exactly one more turn. The game then ends and scoring occurs.
0-5: horrible. 6-10: mediocre. 11-15: honorable. 16-20: excellent. 21-24: amazing. 25: legendary. Score equals the sum of the highest card in each firework pile.
Public area where discarded and failed-play cards go. All players can examine it at any time. Critical for tracking which cards are still available.
An agreed-upon unspoken rule that gives hints extra meaning. Not official rules but widely used. Example: number hint means play, color hint means save.
Advanced technique where a hint to one player indirectly tells another player what to play. Requires the deducing player to trust the hint-giver's logic.
Knowledge about what a card is NOT, derived from hints. If a hint identifies 3 of 5 cards as blue, the other 2 are known to not be blue.
Five optional variant cards that add a sixth rainbow color. Counts as all colors for hint purposes. Increases difficulty significantly.
